speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, anything holds you up while you're pretty serious. Then on top of that, the greater Lambda Livestock Company, who we used to deal with, they have their men along with the train. And they plan on getting into the Chicago market on a certain day because of price fluctuations, you know. And that's the reason for a lot of that stuff on sheep, especially in the fall when they ship the lambs. So everything has to click, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About how often do they have to get off to be watered?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Every 18 hours.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Of course, what we had to do was do a load them at Bovill. We had to make Missoula, Montana. It's the only feeding spot. When you're held up someplace, that's pretty bad. That's the reason we had to unload them at Palouse. We had to load them, feed them down there so as the Northern Pacific could get them into Missoula in 18 hours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess that to be in the railroad business, you've got to be exact and on time. Yeah, well, there's a lot of things involved, but it's just part of the game.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I've got a lot of information that I have found, except yourself, who was, I think, so closely involved with the potlatch management, who is still around. And one question that I would like someone who is closer to the management to talk about is the background of warehouse or interests coming to Idaho from the Midwest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's your understanding of the process that was involved there?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I think this will answer that question.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: The Decker article here.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I know they came out a few years before you did.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That kind of goes into all the cutting out of the timber in Wisconsin and Minnesota. And all these stockholders had their money in that thing back there. And when a timber is cut out, that's why Mr. Weyerhaeuser used to call it sugaring off. That's when they make their money, winding the thing up.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So when they quit back there, these stockholders had plenty of money, and they weren't looking for investments. And they sent their cruisers out into Idaho here, and that was the way they got out here. The Weyerhaeusers didn't own all this timber by any means. They were owned by various groups of people. But the Weyerhaeusers manufactured it for this group. I see. I see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there an unmet demand at the time they came out here? Was there becoming a shortage of timber in the Midwest to fill the new economy?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, it was just opening up out here at that time.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And a lot of this stuff, they had no idea of the value of this timber out in this country to start with until they got their cruisers out here and got to looking around. Then they threw their money into the picture. For instance, the Clearwater timber company, that timber was held down there for 20 years, I guess, after it was bought before the Los Angeles started.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: They had bought way along ahead. The same morsel brought the coast, and the west coast, the Tacoma. That was, well, I guess they're still buying. The side -hoast stuff was, well, the first, they went into Bonners Ferry and Sand Point. That was their first operations in Quine Lane. They bought their first timber up there. That's what started in Idaho, because they got into this Idaho white pine, which is a very valuable timber, and very few stands of it anymore around the country. This part of Idaho is the only place it grows.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Biscuit. Just that one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know, was there much competition at the time in the Idaho to get ahold of the buildings?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: These stockholders, they were pretty much all grouped together, and they all threw their money into a pot, and they had certain timber companies that they invested in. But in the end, a warehouse were manufactured it for them. Oh, a warehouse were owned some timber themselves, too, but never most of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These other interests, are they, for example, the Lairs and the Nortons, and those people, or are they part of the, are they just?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, Lairs, the Nortons, and the Thatcher's, well, all, they were all, they all had retail lumber yards along the Mississippi River.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Lochman's, and all those, Director's Meeting was quite a group. But there was no very dissension among them.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: They trusted Warehauser, and he's the man that kept the timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about when the first cruisers came into this part of Idaho looking at the timber?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, I don't. I do not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Derry among the first? Huh? Was Derry one of the first men who came out here?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, there were cruisers out here before Derry.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Bill Helmer, and I fella down the Clearwater.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, what the heck was his name?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I've forgotten now. But they were all early day pioneers out here. Bill Derry came out here to build sawmills. He put the thing in operation. He was only one woman, of course. But he came out after the timber, a large amount of the timber had been bought.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And he formed the St. Joe Boom Company and all that kind of stuff. Built Potlatch and Elk River and Coeur d 'Alene.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was most of the timber bought from the state or bought from private individuals in this area?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Indeed, I don't mean exactly, but.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: A part of it was, and a part of it was individual holdings. There was individuals that owned timber, still owned timber that they couldn't sell, and that they lost their shirt, of course, because they couldn't get it logged.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: There was one other interest besides the warehouses, which was a group of Pennsylvania lumbermen that opened up Spirit Lake. Oh, yes, you mentioned that to me when I was here. Yeah, that was an entirely different group. They were lumbermen out of Pennsylvania. Well, it was the same idea that they run out of timber back there, got cut out, and they wanted to invest, and so they brought their money west. And they were, shall we say, late comers. They come in late, and they didn't get there as much, but they got all that timber off Spirit Lake.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you know about the decision to build the railroad, the WI&M, in the way that it was built as far as where it goes, the loop that it takes?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, to start with, for the lumber company set up their first mill in Palouse, you know. But they soon found out that they didn't have enough room down there for the size of the plant that they wanted to build, so they came up to Potlatch, where they bought land that was cheap, and they bought that whole site for the town site and mill and storage and everything else. That's what established Potlatch. Well, there was no railroad at that time. The northern Pacific had built into Palouse, and the lumber company wanted the northern Pacific to build from Palouse up into the timber. In other words, where the WI&M goes, no. But they couldn't make them see the value of a railroad into the timber. We couldn't induce them to build. They were invited to build their railroad. So, build area, the hell with you, we'll build our own. That's kind of a fellow you know. So, we built our own WI&M. We followed the drainage areas, which is, well, I think that's the way most of the railroads are built. They followed the drainage areas,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: the valleys, that's where they got to put their railroad, same as the highways. And we followed up to only one way to get to Potlatch, of course, just follow the Palouse River up. We followed that to Harvard. Then we had to get into this other ownership up toward Bovill, off -rovers, so we had to go over the divide into the Potlatch drainage area. We followed that area into Bovill. Their intent was to build on into Montana.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But about that time, the Milwaukee started building west, and the Milwaukee built from St. Mary's down to Bovill, and we induced them to build from Bovill to Elk River. So, I stopped the building up, WI&M any further than it was. But it was built and conceived as a bumper road, just the kind of it is. And we wanted different connections. We had connections at both ends, Milwaukee at one end, and Great Northern and Northern Pacific at the other. We had to do that in order to get into the different territories of selling our lumber. We had our own sales company at that time. And of course, during those days, our most good bit of our lumber went into the building up of the west, that is the west as far as Dakota's and Montana's concerned. A great deal of our lumber went into that area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But does that get built up while we start branching out and just clear to the east coast?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So we had to have good railroad connections in order to do that.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And we did, the railroad was a very good and prosperous railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The length of time that it took to construct the railroad, do you know about how long? How long it took?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I think it started in 1905, in about two years, in 1907 I think. The saw mill started in 1907, and we had the railroad built as far as Harvard. We was getting our timber out of the Harvard area. And we kept on building. We didn't build it, we had a railroad construction outfit built for us. We paid them for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was this outfit?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Do you remember who they, then that's something I had never known, that it was. Well, I forgot the name of the outfit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The people that did the building, the crews, there were a lot of foreign workers among them, weren't there?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, these big construction crews, yes, sure. That's all we had out here. We got mostly Japanese to start with, and Chinese. They were the laboring people nearly days in the west, even in the mines and stuff like that. They were all orientals.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I've heard of Italians also in the area working out there.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Then as the saw mill started operation, the Swedish people followed west from Minnesota. They were saw mill people, and woods people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That's the band, yeah, most of our, well, take these formative we got there, all of the Swedes, they had to be able to talk Swedish, you know?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And the same way as our saw mill, the part on the part that showed about half Swedes that I came there, they were saw mill men from back in Minnesota. They followed the lumber industry out.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And they were very good, reliable people too. That's one thing that Potlatch to start with, was blessed with, was a good, honest, reliable people. They took an interest in their work.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: The company took an interest in them too. That's the reason we had a company town for one reason.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We, Mr. Werhauer told me once, he says, demo, I want a good school run here, says our men and our children are entitled to a good education. That was in the company town, of course. The reason he told me that, I was chairman of the school. But the foremen, the different heads of different outfits had to take the place of like city council and school and all that kind of stuff in a company town.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So, because the company run it, you know?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And each one of us had, my lot came to the school.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And we did have one of the best grade high schools in the state of Idaho.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We run that the same as Della Sano.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was that?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We run it, the teachers didn't run it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was, what makes you say or know that it was of such good quality, the school? What makes you say that it was of such good quality in the school?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, because we made it that way and we paid higher money for good teachers than these other towns are out or even Moscow. But we reserve the right to fire and hire teachers as if they didn't fit the bill too. The teachers weren't telling us what to do as they are now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, who made up your board, the school board, besides yourself?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, I had two other men on. One was the cashier of the bank and the other was the land agent for the lumber company. We had to comply with the laws of Idaho, of course. We had a little election that nobody voted that. Vaught twenty -two voted, elected these three men every year. And I'm not a school man, but I run the school. And the way I run it was this. I hired a superintendent to do as he was told kind of a school we wanted to run. And every year, well, the Idaho Board of Education will send the group in from Boise to see if we qualified for Idaho schools. And besides that, there's a group of instructors in Portland, I think, the Northwest Educational Association. They sent, we hired them to send a group of three men in every two years to check our curriculum and our teachers and see if they were getting good education. And on top of that, every five years, we sent to Iowa State and got that group to come out here. They spent a week with us, five men, looking over our schools. Now, that's how I run the school. I didn't know about this, but I hired men that could tell me what to do. And the company paid the bill. That's the reason we had a superior school.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We built the schoolhouse ourselves. We furnished them ourselves. The people paid no school taxes of any kind.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And there's some of these other towns around here are a little bit jealous of us, but that doesn't make any difference to us. We did that because of our men, and we wanted to keep them happy. We did, we had good family relationships. And the school was one of them. Store was another, like you buy things at the company store, cheaper than you could over here in Moscow. But as I look back on that now, all that stuff,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: people gradually take all those things for granted. They never miss it until they have to go someplace else to find out.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That seems like human nature. The grass is always greener. Yes, that's right, that's right.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But it did hold our men. They were happy to live in Potlatch. They lived the way they wanted to. They had cheaper rents. They had a place else, the United States, electric, telephone, everything else. We owned all that stuff.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And we owned our own hospitals, even the churches.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So really the company pretty much provided for the social life and the educational life and all phases of life for people.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was all a part of different outfits duties, part of their duties on their job. I afterwards, of course, as you know, I afterwards got out of the school business and got into politics and taxes for the lumber company. But again, that was a part of my job other than running an railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Mr. Laird very deeply involved in the running of the town of Potlatch?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, 100%, yes, yes. Very, very strict disciplinary end. But he wanted everything done for the benefit of the employee. But he was very strict. And first, nobody can sell liquor in Potlatch or anything like that. I don't say it wasn't brought in, but.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, and oh, he took an interest this way. Just an illustration of how you asked the question, how he's interested. At one time, to start with, he was the head of the schools. Of course, this thing grew so much that he had to get rid of some of those jobs. But to start with, he was the head of the schools.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And he hired, he interviewed every teacher that was hired. The superintendent hired a teacher, but the next comes he was to Laird.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Every, for every teacher that was hired was a Sunday school teacher too.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, that was a part of their job, extra job, to keep the church going. We had three churches and a Catholic church and a little sweet church and a church for everybody else. They all, the company built them and heated them very slight water for them, but the people had to take care of the minister's salary and all that. Anything else they spent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what was Mr.Laird's title there in his?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Mr. Laird was General Manager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He succeeded Bill Derry?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: He succeeded Bill Derry. When Bill Derry was here, he was General Manager and Laird was Assistant General Manager. No. The Laird was not a lumberman. He was a banker, but he knew enough to hire a good lumberman. Bill Derry was a lumber logger, a lumberman. Laird was very successful in running the company. He had a very good reputation out among the other lumbermen and other people he had to contact. I think if you'd go up around Spokane and find the men that were instrumental in building Cooley Dam and the Ilden Railroad and all that kind of stuff, you'd find that they'd say, Mr. Laird was a very fine gentleman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When Bill Derry and Mr. Laird were both there working together, what was the division of responsibility?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Was Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Laird's responsibility the town?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, Laird took out the financial end of it, yes.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Bill Derry didn't think about figures. Didn't want to, but all the, and Laird looked, I think he, I don't recall it, he had charged the sales department too, but not the manufacturing end of it, or the logging end of it. But we had our own bank and he had charges out, he had charges at town site, he had charges of the taxes and the politics and everything else. He took care of all that.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I take it too that he was probably rather well educated.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: He was a little better educated than Bill Derry was, yes. I don't know the extent of his education, I don't know that.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Bill Derry, I always hear people talk about him, they always say, boy, he was a rough, tough lumberjack.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm wondering what his character was like. Was he a very blunt man, very direct? Was he short with people?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: He was one of these self -made men. He had to fight for a living when he was a kid, I imagine, because, well, he was brought up as a lumberjack in the woods of Minnesota, Wisconsin, and on the river, and the sawmills. He knew these things by first -hand knowledge.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Planning ahead, I doubt if he was very capable of anything of that kind. He knew what kind of saw money he wanted to build, and all that kind of stuff. He knew what was, what could make money and what couldn't. Only he made one heck of a mistake when he built Elk River.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was that a mistake here?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, the lotus could have been brought into potlatch and cut in lumber a lot cheaper than they did to Elk River. First place, Elk River is a snow belt, and it was pretty tough operating up there, very expensive. Matter of fact, that was the days before dry kills, and it was so wet, and the snow lasted so long that you couldn't get the lumber dried in the yard, piled it in the yard to dry. All that was a complete flop.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the reason that the mill lasted such a short time there?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yes, yeah. It really wasn't turning a profit to justify the expensive operation.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh yes, it was an expensive operation, and it should never have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did Bill Derry take advice from many people, or did he make all the decisions himself?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Himself.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Then after... He'd give a man a job to do. That's the last he'd ever hear of Bill Derry, unless the job went somewhere, then you'd hear it from right now. But he didn't want anybody to come in and bother him about it, or anything of that kind.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And he... They didn't make money on that St. Joe Boom Company up there at Coeur d 'Alene.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But it took that kind of a person to put the thing across to start with. You'll find, I think you'll find the same type of man in the early days of mining in this country. Practically the same kind of a man. These early -day men that developed some of these mines.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I mean, a pioneer or somebody who wouldn't let anything... obstacles stand in his way like... That's right.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of... Confidence do you think that the...warehouse or interests had in Derry? Was it a complete confidence? Did he make all these decisions?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, yeah, yeah, she bet. Yeah. Uh -huh. He wasn't... expected to explain or represent why he was doing what he was doing very much.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, he... No, they placed all the confidence in the world in the bill, especially in the timber area. Logging.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And he's one of these fellas that... can fight at the drop of the hat. He was really pretty rough.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: You wouldn't think of somebody in higher level of management having to fight about anything anyway. He's not going to have somebody coming up and giving.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or wasn't it like in the old days, when that kind of thing happened more than it would today?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, you... The kind of men he was dealing with, you had to... They had to know that he could take care of himself, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a big man, physically?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Not tall, but he was big, yes, yes.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Now... For instance, Bill used to say, oh, the early days, Saturday night, you'd come and you'd get on horses and ride the pollution or something, you'd get on to a dance and... Do a little raking and one thing or another. They used to, well, they used to run a passenger train from here to Palouse on a Sunday to bring the crew home from Saturday night.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, Bill's idea was this. He says, if you want a drink, get the hell out of town, go do it in town.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And that was his way of expressing himself with just so many words, too, and he mattered. Same way with Princeton up here. He was pretty rough -tolerant one time. And we had... We had to keep our men out of there, if we could.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And of course, construction, real construction, used to be pretty rough stuff all the way.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, he hired a man that built sawmills. He told one kind of a sawmill he wanted and a man named Horst Cutty built it.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And he built two or three mills for the company, matter of fact. He built the... He built Cornell Lane, he built Albert.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I don't think he was in on long view, I don't believe so. But he was a sawmill construction man and of course, they were dealing with men of that kind. And he was a good man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mill go up fast from when they started to when they could start?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah, they do what they want, of course. All they had to do was put it together. Of course, they owned this sawmill down at Palouse and they sawed all our timbers and everything for this mill down at Palouse. We used to ship the locks down the river to Palouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it the largest sawmill in the world?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: It was the largest white pine sawmill in the world at one time, yes. There were larger sawmills on the coast because the requirements over there for the handling larger timber. But as far as white pine is concerned, it's the largest white pine sawmill.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, talking about the character of the country is being pretty tough then. I've heard that the conditions were pretty tough too in the camps when they first started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the camps?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah. Well, yes, they were because they didn't know anybody.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We improved all things and all lines of work while we improved our camp conditions too. But I think to start with, I would say that the camps, the Partlatch Woods were about the same as they were in the Wisconsin and Minnesota woods. Matter of fact, they were patterned after that. And they were pretty crude, yeah, that's right. But in the end, they were pretty good too. They had very good living conditions in the woods when I left.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was just a case of natural improvements, you know.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Working conditions of all kinds have improved over a period of years. That's all I wanted too.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: The men that worked constructing that came into the country, it seemed like so many of them just disappeared. No trace of them anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the story on that? What kind of construction you ever approached?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I'm thinking of the building of the railroad, the main.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, naturally, the Transcontinental Lines, they followed the line of rails one end or the other. And they never, as a matter of fact, there were temporary camps all along the whole building of the road. On the WIM here, we had a camp at Potlatch to start with. The next one was at Harvard, the next one was Yale, the next one was at Derry. We set those camps up for maybe six month duration or something of that kind, you know.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I wanted to try to talk to someone who was Italian who had been working in the camp. I found one old Greek who had stayed in Elk River, Dementus, I don't know if you knew John Dementus, but they're these foreigners, Eastern Europeans, and certainly the Chinese or Japanese laborers are just terribly hard to find. Well, that's first from the, Potlatch Bill first started.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We had a contractual agreement with the Japanese agency in Seattle to furnish us with labor. And we had a regular Japtown at Potlatch, so it was trying to separate from the rest of the town. In other words, we didn't want to get the people mixed up. And they would even, this fellow of course,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: he was a rustless, jap, he'd bring a bunch of men in and he'd tell them what to do and that was that. They didn't do what was right, because we'd send them back and oh, he was tough with them. And all our groceries had to be bought through him and all that kind of stuff. He just said he had a monopoly on labor, on Jap labor. Of course, we got rid of that as soon as we possibly could. It was replaced with Greek labor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do? What was the laboring work that they were supposed to do in Potlatch? Common labor?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, there's a lot of common labor around the sawmill. Just a lot. Gosh, all the, now gauge track work to keep up through there, gauge lumber road, buggies in the lumber yard. We had miles of it in there. And any of this common labor stuff. There was common labor in the sawmills and the firehole and all that kind of stuff. Oh, just a lot. On the railroad, our section crews, they were all Japanese. We dealt with that Jap for a while.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Do you know about when the - Yeah.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Once a month, a payday. The four women committed to Spokane going to Jap Town. Stayed for three nights.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they Japanese, these women?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know about when they went out and you got Greeks in, take their place?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Mm, I was a gradual thing. The end of the Japs was of course during the war. We had to let them all go. But they had been down to Pigner and Nothing before that. There were a few left. Oh, I had, it was a Jap, Jallers are at the store and there was a Japanese gardeners. And no, there was probably a dozen when the war broke out. But they all had to get out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they mix very much with the townspeople when they were there?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, no, no.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Although the kids went to school, they were all right. They had to get in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of them speak English, the adults?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: The kids did, the parents didn't, all those. We had one that the Jap Jallers, the store, there's some questions about the fellow. Whether or not he might have been sent in here. Of course, everybody was under suspicion during the war. But this fellow in particular, I think he was planted here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean as some kind of an agent?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: All that feeling ran pretty strong during the war.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: It got quite a bit of feeling at one time, yeah, on those fellows.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a similar feeling about Eastern Europeans at that time too?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Because it seemed like a lot of them, I've been told, disappeared at World War I. They thought they went back to Eastern Europe. The Germans, yeah, the Germans and the Italians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the management view of all the IWW agitation that was going on?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was the start of unionism.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was the start of the unions coming into the woods. It started with the war. And IWW was a kind of a, oh, it was a fringe organization. It was

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: mostly to see how much commotion they could cause. I think it was a part of the war deal to start with. But they got into the woods and they were pretty strong at one time. And we had quite a bit of trouble with them, yes. And they developed into a labor organization. And of course, they didn't last very long as such because they were too radical.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say part of the war deal, are you thinking, meaning that they were part of the opposition to World War I and are fighting in the war?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do mostly?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Was it, I've heard a lot of different things about what they may have done or didn't do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did they disrupt? Did they stop?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: They were mostly located in the woods, in the woods operation. And they would, all their drive nails and logs and stuff like that, you know, and you couldn't find them, put them through the band mill. The first thing you know, you'd rip the teeth off a band saw. And things of that kind. They were destructive, no question about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ever strike the camps in the woods? Ever what? Did they strike the camps in the woods around here?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, not exactly a strike, but they were infiltrated in there that we had to send some of our own men in to combat it. Mostly talk and this little things that I say, drive nails and logs, they'd induce somebody to think that he was being mistreated, you know, and anything to get members.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have much influence in potlatch as compared to what they had in the woods? Did they have much influence on it?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, no, they didn't.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, no, we had a, we had an organization in potlatch.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, we were armed, pretty near a National Guard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that, did it, what did that group do?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We organized that on the face of it to train men who were going to be drafted into the Army. And we had an ex -West Pointer there that got kicked out of the Army, but he was a good drill master. And we had quite a company there. Oh, I'd want to it. And we'd drill every so many nights a week. And, but it was, to start with, it was started out to these young fellows that were susceptible to the draft. And by the way, most of those fellows who were drafted were taken into the regular Army as corporals. They had that much military training and they, we were proud of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have standing as a National Guard unit or?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, we bought our own guns and the company bought them.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And we belonged to the National Rifle Association.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That did have its influence on keeping the IWWs out of potlatch. It did, definitely.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And there were a fair number of the, of the members of the group were, were sawmill workers.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh yes, yes, yes. Just the citizens of potlatch.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And what happened,

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did this, the 4L begin as a, the 4L as a?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was the first real labor organization that developed, that was to combat the IWW, to start with. We had, there was a man that was connected with the, all the lumber companies. He organized that thing over at Seattle. And that was to combat the IWW. And of course it developed into a labor organization. I don't see any existence now because it was, it was conceived and organized by the lumber companies.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Did it get, It was a very popular organization too.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: It would give them a chance to belong to something so that an organizer couldn't come in and call him a scab.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And it was, it served its purpose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, did that become the labor union of the mill and potlatch at the time?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And of course, they, I think, see, I think we had a strike, 4L strike. And that kind of fizzled out, then that was about the end of the 4Ls. And the national organizations came in after that.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was a striking potlatch. I know, I know, Hap Moody told me about an early, some early strike when he was dead. Oh yeah, Hap was, Hap was sheriff and he came over and deputized.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I was a deputy sheriff. We were all deputized.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that part of that 4L strike you think that?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah. When Mr. Laird became the general manager, is that about when T .P.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Jones became part of the company operation?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, Jones came out here with Dury.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he'd been around?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: He came out with Dury from back around Chippewa Falls or someplace where he was, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Since Laird didn't know so much about the woods operation, did more of that fall on Jones's shoulders?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah, he was Wood's superintendent. Yeah, there was,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: he was in charge of the woods, no question about that, yeah.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Mm -hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, as far as the way decisions were made on the running and operation of the railroad, how did the chain of command work? How did the chain of command work for the running and operation of the railroad?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I assume as general manager of the railroad, you were at the top and had to final say on the basic decisions of the railroad.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, the railroad was entirely separate corporation from the lumber company. No connection between them at all. Well, there's a connection in that the lumber company paid for the building of it and then took stock and bonds for their expenditure because so they owned the stock and bonds of the railroad company, the lumber company did.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But we had to operate under the rules, the interstate commerce commission, the same as any other railroad. And of course, that was one of the things they finally had to find out the hard way that they had to get some money in and do something about a railroad. To start with, the first year of the operation of the railroad, they owned a bunch of lumbermen and some of the people left over from the construction crew and it was kind of a haphazard thing. But they soon found that they had to.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: You told me before that some guys got thrown in jail for not knowing the way things ought to have been done. Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was wondering, what were the kind of violations that you could get into if you didn't know the way to do things?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, for us, it's a site one illustration. The first place we built our stations and we built grain warehouses at each station and we run these warehouses, grain warehouses. Well, we soon found that we should get out of the warehouse business. So we leased the warehouses to a man named Mark P. Miller, here in Moscow. He owned a flour mill there and he run the grain warehouses. Well, we didn't pay much attention to Mark until one day an interstate communist man came in and says we're you're indicted, Mark Miller's indicted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What for?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, Mark was shipping stuff out of Derry that and misquoting it. He didn't, he had stuff in the car and he shouldn't have and didn't declare it and was getting it shipped over to Moscow at a cheap rate. Well, of course, we didn't know anything about it because Carlo'd ship it, we never expect. And so we got all of that went all right, but Mark P. Miller got, then we had a superintendent to start with. He bought a, he wanted to go farming. He bought a farm up around Harvard and build a house, barns and everything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the interstate communist inspector come in one day and says, what you gonna do about this?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: The old man's shipping stuff up there free.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So he was canned. And those are the things, yeah, those are.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Conflict of interest.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Of course, I think perhaps in the early days, the first couple of years, there was a lot of that stuff done. Well, it's our railroad, we can do all the running with it. But they soon found out they couldn't do that with the railroad.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the personnel structure of the WI&M when you were the general manager? What was the chain of command within the railroad? Who were the?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, I had a master mechanic that took care of the mechanical work. I had a, I had a master mechanic dispatcher who took care of the operation of the trains. And I had a road master who looked after the maintenance of the road and the telephone company. We owned the telephone company in Potlatch, the railroad owned that and also owned the line from Potlatch to Arbogal to Palouse.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So I had to have a combination, man. I knew something about telephones and well, I made him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the various depot agents? Were they directly under you or were there someone else in charge of them?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, the auditor took care of the depot agents.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: They come under the supervision of the auditor and we had an auditor. I'd forgotten about him. That's so evil.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, I shouldn't have said that.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: You gotta have fiscal management. Someone's gotta.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So I had, I had my work pretty well distributed and I, all I had to do was after I got it organized was supervise it. I had had a lot of experience before I came here, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who went into that last time?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: You know, the size of the trains is something I was wondering about. I saw one picture labeled longest log train in the world. A hundred car log train, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the story on that particular train?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, they just made that up for something to talk about. That wasn't a usual train. No, no.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And there was a lot of that stuff that was,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: shouldn't have been done, of course.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: When I was operating, our trains would consist of about 60 cars. That was my opinion, good operation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you're talking about a hundred cars?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, that was just a build up.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We had a, we had a, one of the better types of short line railroads.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: There's something like over 350 short line railroads in the country. Most of them are in connection with some big industry, you know, like Christmas connection with the lumber company. Others are connection with the steel companies and the oil companies and what have you. Just a lot of them, 350 of them. They have quite an organization, America's Short Line Railroad Association. I have a president in Washington. I have a Washington office. I was vice president of the organization for some little time. And yeah, it's quite an institution. One short line railroad don't have very much to say when you're talking to these big lines. But 350 of them, if Poppas and Mamas like lumber companies and steel companies have quite a bit to say from political standpoint. And it was a very good organization, well -founded. We were not radical.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And we, well, we had to help our less fortunate short lines. We had to send a man here and there to kind of get him straightened out about something, you know, things of that kind. Because a lot of them were, you know, they didn't just didn't know what to do, I'll operate.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Matter of fact, I had a letter from a fellow back in Wisconsin the other day. They had the Libby Owens Glass Company, one of the biggest doctors in the United States, owns a short line railroad back there. This fellow sent me a little folder that they got out and told them about the railroad. It's very interesting.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: You talked to me a little about being in the legislature representing the North Idaho lumber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it, the North Idaho lumber?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: North Idaho Forestry Association.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah, I worked for them.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: One thing that you talked about that happened before then was the creation of the forestry law for the state of Idaho, the writing of that law. Well, there was no connection between those things. Yeah. Yeah. That's right.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I was wondering about... This North Idaho Forestry Association was set up as a kind of a fact -finding organization for all the lumber companies of North Idaho. And they would meet every so often and express their opinions about maybe legislation or taxation or things of that kind. It had nothing to do with operation of lumber companies. But the nice thing about it was,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: as far as politics and taxes were concerned, there was no dissent. When I go to Boise, I was talking for all of them. Nobody... there was nobody telling me, well, I don't believe in this. I believe it's up and else. They were all in the court. It was easy to work for a plan of that kind. So they ironed out their difficulties in their office before it was headed to me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: While you were representing them, what was the general nature of the interests, the kinds of legislation that they would favor, the kind that they would be opposed to?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, on the taxes, timberland lumber taxes and sawmill taxes are pretty terrific. And we'd meet three times a year. I had to go to Boise at least three times a year to meet with the Assessors Association, if you please, the County Assessors Association. They had a lot to say about how some of this stuff should be taxed. I met with them. I went down in every August to the Board of Equalization, Tax Board of Equalization meeting, at which time you can express yourself as to what your taxes are and should be, compared to something else and all that kind of thing. So there was a lot on the taxes. Just as far as legislation was concerned,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I was down there. I had two lawyers with me, one all the time and the other only part -time. One was a labor lawyer, but he was only there part -time. The other was a general counsel, Coeur d 'Alene. He kept me advised on the legality of things. And my job was to see that no adverse legislation was put through that would harm the lumber interest. And once in a while, we had something we wanted to promote, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And now it's a form of lobbying, all right?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: There's no question about that, although I never ask a legislator to vote or not to vote for anything that I had thought of. You can't control legislation that way. I set up offices down there. More of an information bureau than anything else. And it takes a while to gain their confidence, but once you do that, they come to you and ask you how a bill will affect lumber companies. Most of the legislators don't want to do anything that will affect the industry and the state, you know. And if they can trust somebody, they'll come to them for their information, because they don't have time to get it themselves. They don't have time to read all these bills that are put through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you had to keep abreast of the legislation?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, yes, yes, yes, yes. You probably had to research it yourself in some cases. Mm -hmm.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So, on the whole, we had a pretty good setup. At least I was never criticized. The fellow that followed me took my place, so I quit. He got halfway through the session, and they called him before the state senate and told him to get the hell out of town.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, the poor kid, he just didn't... He was just overpowered with his authority and all. Which we said.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there interests in the state that ran in conflict with the lumber company interests as far as, let's say, promoting the legislation that you had to oppose?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yes, there were. There were the...

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Oh, you take the mines and the railroads

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: and the telephone companies, power companies. We all have certain things in common. All like, say, safety rules.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Work with compensation. And all these social security things, they're all... affect all of us the same way. So, we had the... what was called a self -insurers. That's the self -insurers of the... We owned our own hospitals and our doctors and stuff like that. And when the state law says that we have to protect our men. So, we formed the self -insurers because our interests were all common. We worked through that organization for legislation along that line. Now, we didn't work on anything that would be... ...competitive with somebody else or anything like that. No, no, we just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you give me an example of the kind of legislation that you had to oppose? Because it would have hurt the lumbering industry in the state?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yeah, well, sure.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Just comes to mind. This is kind of a funny one, too.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I was opposing a bill that the drug people had introduced in the Idaho legislature.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: All like the selling of aspirin in drugstores and stuff like that, you know. And all those minor things. They wanted to be a little more strict. You couldn't sell them in drugstores or grocery stores. And they had quite a bill. And I started to fight again. And I got a call one night about, oh, after midnight, something opened my door.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A gamble?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yes. It says, well, I'm so -and -so from Seattle. Well, I said, I don't know you. Well, he says, I represent the drug company. He says, I'd like to talk to you. So I got up and let him in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He says, what the hell is a lumber company that's opposing our drug bill for?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, I says, I'll tell you. I says, you know, we run camps out in the woods. Maybe 250 men in a camp. We have to keep a certain supply of different things in that camp. Old heavy clothing and tobacco. And among other things, aspirin and drugs of certain kinds that we dare handle. Cough drops and things of that kind. We have to have that in our supplies at these camps. I says, your bill would prohibit that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, he says, who the hell would ever have thought of that?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, that's an illustration of one thing.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Another is a different angle. Oh, good, many years ago, we had an infestation of tussock moth. We've got another one now. In the woods. And it was something new in this country. And it got away from us. We lost a lot of timber. Died overnight. So the man from the federal government came out here and says, we got to spray. He says, we have to spray that will kill that. And they got to hire planes and spray the whole damn woods. Everybody's going to lose their trees. National government, the state government, lumber companies. Individuals, everybody. Says, it's got to be done and done fast. All right. We got the lumber companies to agree. And we got the state to agree. The federal government to agree. I'd have to go through Washington. This all takes time. And we had to rush a bill through the legislature authorizing the state of Idaho to come in on some basis and spray these trees. And we had to put a bill up that way. That is the only bill that I actually came out flat footed and asked legislators to vote for. And I had to be an agent of the legislators. And I had the biologists from Coeur d 'Alene come down there and give them a talk. I fed them. Give them what they wanted to drink. Made a party out of it.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And that had to be done that way and done fast. Now, that was one of the things that we were for. We handled it that way. It was rather a crude way, but you couldn't handle everything that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember about when that was?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I forget what year that was. I really do.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: They come out the other day in the paper. They were supposed to have sprayed last fall. And they said then when the other— I don't remember. I don't know what year that was. That's been quite a while ago.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That's been 30 years ago. You had to have that piece of legislation.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: So that's something that we were for and we're fighting for. We come out flat footed on it.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Was there ever any environmental list, environmental group opposition in the early days to— Not like there is now, to any kind of development.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Nobody knew what environmental meant then.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I was wondering about the maintenance of the railroad and about the kinds of adversities that you had to put up with. One that strikes me that must have caused problems sometimes would be mudslides and the tracks washing out. Well, let's just keep an upper railroad. That's all.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Bridges have to be rebuilt. Sure, I rebalasted the whole railroad while I was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Re -ballasted?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I brought in a contractor from Spokane, crushed rock for a year, and give the track a lift of so many inches and put new ballast under it. When it was built, they tried to—which is all right, I guess— get their rock for ballast as close as possible. Well, the closest they got was a darn poor rock. It was a silica rock and it didn't hold up at all. The first thing you know, we was having trouble. We didn't have enough ballast under our rails. If we wanted to operate fast and heavy trains, so we had to rebalast. So I rebalasted the whole thing. I rebuilt all the bridges, 21 of them. That has to be done every year. Everything that's built of wood has only lasted so long. And it was just part of the railroad and that's all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you rebalasted, where did the rock come from that time?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Right this side of Beaudeville, we got a basalt rock up there.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: This stuff they got here is all up at Yale. It's all wet and pounding it into the— under the ties that'll dis -intrigaze. First thing you got is just kind of a soapy -like stuff.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: The same way with a big fill we had that went out. I was down in Palouse one day and sectioned form and called me up and says, No trains today.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Fill bridge number so -and -so slid out. Well, what had happened, they had that— before I was in charge, we had a road master. He was a civil engineer too. And he decided that some of the bridges could be filled, cheaper than renewed. And he talked the boss into it and he filled them. There again, he took his fill material as close as he could get it, you know, to make it cheap. And he come into this silica again, as slippery as could be when it's wet. Well, you notice some of the concrete in Moscow here. And this was a bridge that was about 80 feet high

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: and 500 feet long, he filled it. And what had happened, he had left the piling in the trestle and he filled it with dirt. And he put ballast on top of it. And he had a filled bridge. Fine, looked good. Had a concrete culvert under it. Over the years, as the snow fell and the rains came down, it would go in the top of this thing in the ballast, you know, the ballast would let water through. And this darn fill would hold that water like a pail, if you please. It got clay. And it got to the point where it became saturated, cleared onto the base. The top weight was too heavy for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Just went out like that. The whole damn works.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what to do?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Gee, guys. Had a sawmill to keep going, the witch department to keep going. So he put in a steam shovel and some dump cars and started filling with rock. It took us two weeks to do it, but we did it. And the mill kept going. Didn't have to shut it down because we had enough locks. But those are some of the things that happened.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That sounds like a real crisis situation. Well, it was. It was. And it was a,

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: something that hadn't happened around this country before. And we had a lot of railroad men from other railroads come in to take a look at the thing to see what was happening. And it was quite a problem. They was taking it home to their own railroads because they had done the same thing.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But it all slipped now, I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your attitude as a general manager toward the people that worked for you? Was there attitude?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Your attitude towards what you expected from them and...

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I'd never ask a man to do anything I couldn't do myself. I wouldn't do myself. I was all, I never criticized a man in front of other men.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I had a crew of men that most of them never worked for anybody else in their life.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Talked to a man out here at a funeral the other day. A gentleman says, we looked back to the time we worked for you, he says we're glad of it. I know how to treat men. I know what to expect of them.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I treat them fair. I never had any trouble. I like to make my own men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean by that?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, I've taken kids that couldn't work for anybody else and made damn good railroad men out of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How does that work?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Do you give them responsibility or...

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: No, I train them right. Yes, I give them responsibility as they are capable of accepting it, sure.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But I'm not a person to go out and go downtown and see signs in the windows. I want an experienced clerk.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Most times you have to retrain them the way you want things done. Even college students.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Doesn't surprise me.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: What you learn in books isn't something you can take with you and apply a lot of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did you expect your men to like Bill Derry not to come to you? Like Bill Derry, did you expect your men to take care of themselves and not come to you?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: If they, let's say, wanted advice, yes, sure. Sure.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I think the big thing in being a supervisor of men is to know the job yourself. The men recognize that in a supervisor. If you don't know your job, some of the men on the job will be the first ones to find it out. And I never hesitate about taking advice from a man regardless of how lonely he was on the echelon. No, I learn something every day for somebody. You bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember what Potlatch was like to town when you first came there? What it looked like to you? What the activity was like? What your feel of the place?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: You told me before when we talked you didn't expect to stay there very long. But I'm wondering what your impressions of the town were in 1910.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, it was pretty new then. There wasn't very much entertainment. There were no automobiles and no good roads. We had to make our own entertainment.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And we did. In the summertime, we had four baseball teams in Potlatch. There were good ones, too. People took an interest in it.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We conducted dances. We had an amusement hall. Oh, we'd have people come in and speak once in a while. Political talks had Bill Barr in a lot of times. And the hall would be crowded and come up to hear Bill talk. Politics or otherwise. And it was a big dance hall. It was accommodate a lot of people.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: We divided the town up into departments. We'll say like the railroad and the store and the sawmill and the lumber yard and the plenty mill.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And each one of them was responsible for a dance once a week. Every Saturday night I'd have a dance, a big dance. And this group, each group would have to be responsible for decorating the hall, furnishing, and getting the music, and all that kind of stuff. Those were the things we had to do ourselves in order to keep the people entertained.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And it was very successful. That then developed through baseball and other activity of athletics. And we had an old livery barn there. We quit riding horses. And we turned that into a gymnasium. We hired an instructor, if you please. We had a little basketball team there. We had gymnastics and boxing and all that kind of stuff. Then finally the company built this great big gymnasium, one of the finest in the country. We had the instructor there. We held the Idaho State Tennis Championship there one year and had tennis courts. A line of athletic activities all the way through. We had inside gymnastics, indoor baseball, all that kind of stuff. And we did that to keep the people happy and busy.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And I think they were happy. I don't know whether it would be, no, or not. Of course the automobiles made a big change in those things. Not very many automobiles in 1910.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did Pilots have a pretty stable feeling to it as a town when you first came there? Or did it seem like there was a lot of turnover of people coming?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: When I first came there, I came down there to play baseball.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And the actors got mixed up at the railroad. If anybody had told me I was going to stay there as long as I did, I would have told them it was crazy. But the longer I stayed there, the better I liked it. The more I got involved with the people and the company, the better I liked it. And it was just developed into a good place to be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did it happen that you became general manager?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, that's easy.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I came down there to do a certain job. They kept the folks who had to get me. And one thing led to another.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I had had a lot of railroad experience. They knew that. And a lot of people, I can see where a company town is not a good thing.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Men grew up, and children grew up in a town like that, and had no responsibility to government of any kind. They accept everything as is. Now, when this town over here was changed to an open town, they were lost for a long time and maybe still are to a certain extent. They didn't know how to run their schools. They didn't know how to police their town. They didn't know how to keep up the town. And there were sewers and there were water and there were police protection and the whole damn thing. And they had a pretty rough period there for a while. Just because people didn't have to do those things, you know, they didn't know how to do it.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I think the time comes when the town should be made to town, but I think it should come gradually. I didn't believe the way it was done over there, but it had to be done some way. There were a couple other towns in the United States the same way. The town of Pullman, Illinois. Pullman Car Company was a company town. By the way, Potlatch was kind of pattern after that. We had a town down here at Shasta, right beside Shasta. It was a company town, a sawmill town. But it was a terrible comparison. Running down houses and all, the company didn't pay any attention at all. And I can see where a company town could be a big disadvantage. If the company were inclined to take advantage of things, it might be the store, there would be higher prices. Ours was lower prices. The conditions under which people lived, if they didn't pay any attention to that, could deteriorate. To a big extent. We even papered the inside of people's houses over there for them when they wanted it done. We painted the inside of their houses for them. They had them on the outside too, as far as I could.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did this kind of benevolent policy come from?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Was this Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Weyerhaeuser's?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was the principle of the Weyerhaeusers, that's right.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: That was their principle. When I went down to Boise, to the representative, Mr. Weyerhaeuser came to me. He says, Gamley says, I can't tell you anything you want to do or want not to do down at Boise. He says, all I can say is this. He says, whatever is good for the state of Idaho is good for the pilot and slumber company. And I think that spells the attitude of the Weyerhaeusers.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Which Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Weyerhaeuser was this?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Rudolph.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Yep. They were a great bunch of people. They were, well, when the original Weyerhaeusers came over here, they were just common German people, you know, that had a flame for mechanics and operated sawmills. All the boys were brought up the same way. Believe me, they didn't spend money foolishly. But I did the pilot up, for the fun of piloting it up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the Weyerhaeusers maintain close contact with Potlatch and the lumber company here? Were they out here much? Did they have much to do with the oversight?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Sure, they always had their directors meeting at Potlatch. All the directors always came in at Potlatch.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And Rudolph, particularly, well, see, to start with, the old man, Weyerhaeuser, gave a sawmill to each one of his boys.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Charles Weyerhaeuser was supposed to run the Potlatch bill. And Rudolph Weyerhaeuser, the Coital Lane mill. And John Weyerhaeuser, the Coma mill. And Phil Weyerhaeuser, the Lewiston mill. And that was the way it was. But the one at Potlatch, he was on a trip to Europe and died. He didn't last very long at Potlatch, so Rudolph took over.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And they, as a family, the whole family is built around this sawmill. Every one of their family. Even the girls.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: And it's a party. It's a party. It's kind of a tradition with them, in a way.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: I don't know what would happen if one of them would go haywire like some families do. I don't know what position the family would take. It would be a calamity. I know that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say haywire, what do you mean? You think, got rid of the business?

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Well, you've seen a lot of these people that their father had a lot of money and a lot of business and so forth. And didn't do them very much good.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: But what you're saying is that they knew the business and they knew what they were getting.

speaker: W. J. Gamble
words: Mm -hmm. Mm -hmm. The whole family, the second and third generations, have been just the same. All right down the line.
